channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: Trump: 60-Day War Powers Deadline ‘Totally Unconstitutional’
item.description: The White House argues that the U.S.-Iran cease-fire paused the clock.

item.title: Will the Next Fed Chairman Be More Compliant With Trump?
item.description: Kevin Warsh will face intense pressure to cut interest rates.

item.title: ‘Project Hail Mary’ and the Politics of Science Fiction
item.description: Even the most entertaining tale carries a political message.

item.title: Saffron’s Last Gasp
item.description: The Iran war is choking supply of the world’s most expensive spice.

item.title: The Real Origin of the World’s Most Famous Female Serial Killer
item.description: A new book explores the geopolitical scheming that created the blood countess legend.

item.title: ‘Hokum’ Is Haunted by Ireland’s Dark History
item.description: A new horror film reckons with the country’s buried sins—and the women erased by them.

item.title: What in the World?
item.description: Test yourself on the week of April 25: Iran offers to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, the UAE leaves OPEC, and Colombia hosts a conference.

item.title: Two Novels Take on the Post-American Dream
item.description: Plus, more international fiction releases in May.

item.title: Does acupuncture work?
item.description: It seems useful for pain. The jury’s out on everything else

item.title: Labour faces a drubbing in England’s local elections
item.description: The party could lose more than half of the council seats it is defending

item.title: 5 Years Since UK Parliament Recognized the Uyghur Genocide, There’s Been Little Policy Action
item.description: Britain recognized a genocide, then did nothing to stop it.

item.title: Strategic Autonomy or a Pro-US Tilt? India’s Foreign Policy Under Pressure
item.description: The Iran war is forcing India to confront the tension between strategic autonomy and its commitment to the Indo-Pacific framework.

item.title: Israel’s Slow War on the West Bank
item.description: Israeli leaders seem increasingly comfortable with the collapse of the Palestinian Authority.

item.title: China Never Actually Removed Homosexuality From Its Official List of Mental Disorders
item.description: On the 25th anniversary of the Chinese Classification of Mental Disorders Version 3 (CCMD-3), we revisit the widely repeated claim that China “removed” homosexuality from its list of mental disorders in 2001 – and make the case for retiring it.

item.title: The BRICS Climate Stress Test: India’s Opportunity to Reshape Global Climate Finance Architecture
item.description: BRICS is a climate-critical grouping whether it chooses to be or not.

item.title: Has De-Dollarization Begun?
item.description: US President Donald Trump’s military adventures, attacks on long-standing allies, and dismantling of institutions like USAID are eroding the trust on which the dollar’s global primacy ultimately rests. The world’s reserve currency may have already begun its long, slow decline.

item.title: Why China’s Internet Is Reopening the Case Against the ‘Qin System’ of the First Emperor
item.description: In niche corners of the Chinese internet, discussions of the very first dynasty stand in for critiques of today’s China.

item.title: A US-India Partnership for the AI Age
item.description: Although the United States maintains a decisive edge in frontier AI models and high-end compute, China has proven that its capacity for innovation is a force to be reckoned with. To prevent the Chinese government from claiming the strategic high ground, the US needs the talent and data that only India can provide.

item.title: The Deeper Pattern Behind China’s Military Purges
item.description: Xi’s new commanders are the men his last generals blocked.

item.title: After Hungary, Could Mexico Be Next?
item.description: During his 16-year rule, Hungary's Viktor Orbán could plausibly argue that his model delivered order, stability, and rising living standards. But Mexico's government can make no such claim, suggesting that the country might find itself facing a destabilizing political transition sooner than expected.

item.title: Bangladesh’s Air Power: Intentions Meet a Widening Capability Gap
item.description: The Bangladesh Air Force risks becoming a paper tiger, if it isn’t already.

item.title: Why Trump Might Come to Regret the Iran War
item.description: He hoped it would be transformational; instead, it’s just one more round in the conflict.

item.title: The war in Iran has sent American inflation sharply higher
item.description: Our predictive index has reached a sizzling 3.6%

item.title: The Price of Peace With Iran
item.description: Washington must be willing to make uncomfortable concessions.

item.title: The China-US Clash Over Critical Minerals Is an Opportunity for Kazakhstan
item.description: Amid China-U.S. competition over critical minerals, Kazakhstan is positioned to become a key partner for Washington.

item.title: Why China Treats ‘Lying Flat’ as a National Security Threat
item.description: It's not about the economy at all: In a system that defines not struggling as a crime, opting out is a public offense.

item.title: Are the Ryukyu Islands an Overlooked Flashpoint in the Indo-Pacific?
item.description: Considerable attention has been paid to the threat of a Chinese invasion of Taiwan, but analysts have arguably overlooked a more immediate danger facing the Ryukyu Island chain.

item.title: The Real Meaning of the UAE’s OPEC Exit
item.description: The geopolitical realignment goes much deeper than just oil markets.

item.title: The Real Role of a Trump-Xi Meeting
item.description: The summit is unlikely to deliver decisive breakthroughs. Instead, its importance lies in how it helps manage competition under pressure.

item.title: Kazakhstan’s Nuclear Bet to Reach Carbon Neutrality
item.description: Despite plans to go from zero to four nuclear power plants by 2050, Kazakhstan’s strategy comes with significant challenges.

item.title: Colombia’s Anti-Fossil Fuel Conference
item.description: The country is trying to push for a green transition despite gridlock at U.N. climate talks.

item.title: How Many Divisions Has Pope Leo?
item.description: Following the Trump administration's spat with Pope Leo XIV over its misbegotten war with Iran, one is reminded of previous historical episodes when the Vatican offered moral clarity for a world that had been corrupted by cynicism and aggressive authoritarianism. Americans, above all, should heed the message.

item.title: What China Will Learn from Orbán’s Defeat
item.description: After watching Nikita Khrushchev’s repudiation of Stalin lead to a revolution in Hungary, Mao Zedong concluded that under one-party rule, no amount of political liberalization is safe. The defeat of Viktor Orbán in Hungary’s parliamentary elections last month most likely reinforced that lesson for Chinese President Xi Jinping.

item.title: Trump’s Plan B for Tariffs Rests on Shaky Foundations
item.description: The Section 301 case outlined by U.S. trade officials is neither coherent nor defensible.

item.title: Malaysia’s Tech Talent Shortage
item.description: Industry leaders describe a war for talent.

item.title: The Universal Uprising
item.description: Rather than a story of passive suffering, the history of slavery in the Americas is one of rebellion, solidarity, and democratic invention. A recent book traces the depth and creativity of enslaved people’s resistance, whose legacy continues to inspire social-justice movements around the world.

item.title: The Philippines Joins Washington’s Pax Silica
item.description: It's clear what the United States is getting out of its new critical minerals initiative, but less obvious how Manila stands to gain from it.

item.title: Libya’s False Peace
item.description: Libya needs political unity, not Washington’s dealmaking.

item.title: China and America Are Courting Nuclear Catastrophe
item.description: The consequences of Beijing’s weapons buildup.

item.title: When Myanmar Exported Federalism
item.description: After World War II, Burma helped design a federal constitution for Ethiopia and Eritrea, while fighting a civil war with ethnic minorities demanding the same at home.

item.title: 3D-Printed Weapons: An Emerging Problem in Southeast Asia?
item.description: While traditional firearms are currently a more pressing problem, seizures of 3DPFs are on the rise, with worrying implications for the region.

item.title: Cambodian Opposition Leader Loses Appeal Against Treason Conviction
item.description: The Phnom Penh Appeals Court upheld Kem Sokha's 27-year house arrest sentence, while imposing a further five-year travel ban.

item.title: Myanmar’s Ousted Leader Aung San Suu Kyi Transferred to House Arrest: State Media
item.description: The announcement is part of a concerted PR push currently being undertaken by the new "civilian" government in Naypyidaw.

item.title: Manila’s Piecemeal Military Modernization
item.description: For over 30 years, the Philippines has tried – and struggled – to bolster its defense capabilities. What went wrong?
